justification.personality: The film captures his arrogance and disdain, but the book provides much more depth to his manipulative nature and specific interactions (e.g., with the Ministry/Borgin). The film version feels more confined to specific antagonistic moments.
justification.narrative_role: The core function—antagonist to Harry/Weasleys, representative of pure-blood prejudice—is maintained, though the scope of his influence is reduced in the film.
justification.motivations: His core motivations (maintaining status, adherence to pure-blood ideology, fear of exposure) are present, but the book details his political maneuvering (e.g., regarding the Ministry/governorship) more thoroughly.
justification.character_arc: The film shows his fall from grace (being exposed/defeated) but lacks the nuanced, drawn-out political decline seen in the books, making his arc feel more abrupt.

comparative.key_observations: The film successfully captures his villainous essence and key confrontations (Borgin & Burkes, Ministry), but the book provides a richer tapestry of his political maneuvering and character depth.
